topics.title: Implementation of stack using array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL308 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of stack using array

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Stack** is a linear, ordered data structure that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, where elements are inserted and removed from the same end, called the **top** of the stack. In an **array-based implementation**, a stack is realized using a **one-dimensional array** of fixed maximum size, with a single integer variable (`top`) that tracks the index of the most recently inserted element.

The two fundamental operations on a stack are:
- **PUSH** — Insertion of an element at the top of the stack.
- **POP** — Removal and return of the topmost element from the stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The stack implementation using a static array is the **foundational experiment** in the Data Structures Lab. The expected learning outcome is the ability to *design, code, and test* array-based stack operations including boundary-condition handling (`overflow` and `underflow`).

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stack of Plates" Intuition

Imagine a stack of dinner plates in a cafeteria dispenser:

1. You can only **add a new plate** on the **top** of the existing stack — you cannot insert a plate in the middle.
2. When someone needs a plate, they **remove the topmost plate** — the last one placed is the first one taken.
3. If the dispenser is full (no more vertical space), you cannot push another plate — this is **Stack Overflow**.
4. If the dispenser is empty and someone requests a plate, you cannot serve — this is **Stack Underflow**.

This physical intuition directly maps to the array-based stack:
- The **array** is the finite storage rack.
- The **`top` index** points to the current top plate.
- **PUSH** = placing a new plate; **POP** = removing the top plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Life:** LIFO = **L**ast plate **I**n is the **F**irst plate **O**ut.

---

### 📐 Standard Metrics & Boundary Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum stack size | `MAX` or `N` | User-defined constant (e.g., **100**) |
| Initial top index | `top` | **-1** (denotes an empty stack) |
| Overflow condition | — | `top == MAX - 1` |
| Underflow condition | — | `top == -1` |
| Element access | `stack[top]` | Always the topmost valid element |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### ⚙️ Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

#### 🔹 Data Structure Declaration
A stack using an array is typically declared as:
- An **array** of the data type (`int stack[MAX]`) — provides contiguous memory storage.
- An **integer variable** `top` — tracks the current highest occupied index.

#### 🔹 Initialization
- Set `top = -1`. This is the **canonical empty-stack indicator** in KTU board examinations.

#### 🔹 PUSH Operation (Insertion)
The logic proceeds as follows:
1. **Check overflow first:** If `top == MAX - 1`, the stack is full; reject the insertion.
2. **Increment top:** `top = top + 1`.
3. **Insert element:** `stack[top] = item`.
4. Report success.

**Why increment first?** Because `top` is *initially* -1 (empty), the first valid array index is 0. Incrementing before assignment is the only way the first PUSH lands at index 0.

#### 🔹 POP Operation (Deletion)
The logic proceeds as follows:
1. **Check underflow first:** If `top == -1`, the stack is empty; rejection.
2. **Retrieve value:** `item = stack[top]`.
3. **Decrement top:** `top = top - 1`.
4. Return `item`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners specifically award marks for **checking overflow/underflow conditions BEFORE modification**. Omitting the guard is the single most common cause of mark loss.

#### 🔹 PEEK Operation (Optional, but high-frequency)
- Returns `stack[top]` **without** modifying `top`.
- Useful for examining the topmost element without removal.

---

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Pre-condition | Core Action | Post-condition | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PUSH(x)** | `top < MAX - 1` | `top++; stack[top] = x` | `top` increased by 1 | **O(1)** | O(1) |
| **POP()** | `top >= 0` | `x = stack[top]; top--` | `top` decreased by 1 | **O(1)** | O(1) |
| **PEEK()** | `top >= 0` | `return stack[top]` | No change | **O(1)** | O(1) |
| **isEmpty()** | — | `return top == -1` | — | **O(1)** | O(1) |
| **isFull()** | — | `return top == MAX - 1` | — | **O(1)** | O(1) |
| **Display()** | `top >= 0` | Iterate from 0 to `top` | — | **O(n)** | O(1) |

> **Notation used in formula sheet:**
> - `MAX` = declared maximum size of the array (bolded, **scalar constant**)
> - `n` = current number of elements in the stack (where $n = top + 1$)
> - $O(1)$ denotes **constant-time** operation; $O(n)$ denotes **linear-time** operation.

---

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Function call management** in C/C++ compilers (call stack).
- **Undo/Redo mechanisms** in text editors and IDEs.
- **Expression evaluation** and **syntax parsing** in compilers (infix → postfix conversion).
- **Backtracking algorithms** (maze solving, N-Queens, DFS traversal).
- **Browser history navigation** (back button).
- **Memory management** in operating systems (kernel stacks).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Mathematical Analysis of Stack State Transitions

Let $S$ denote the stack and $n$ denote the **current number of elements**. The state of the stack at any instant is fully described by the tuple:

$$
S = \langle \text{stack}[0..\text{top}],\; \text{top} \rangle, \quad \text{where } n = \text{top} + 1
$$

---

### 🔬 Derivation 1: PUSH State Transition

**Initial State:** Stack contains $n$ elements, with `top = n - 1`.

**Step 1 — Boundary check (guard against overflow):**

$$
\text{if } (\text{top} = \text{MAX} - 1) \implies n = \text{MAX} \implies \text{STACK IS FULL} \implies \text{REJECT}
$$

**Step 2 — Increment top pointer:**

$$
\text{top}_{\text{new}} = \text{top}_{\text{old}} + 1
$$

This shifts the writing cursor to the next free slot.

**Step 3 — Write the new element:**

$$
\text{stack}[\text{top}_{\text{new}}] = \text{item}
$$

**Step 4 — Update element count:**

$$
n_{\text{new}} = n_{\text{old}} + 1 \quad \text{(since } n = \text{top} + 1\text{)}
$$

> **Transition invariant:** After a successful PUSH, the relation $n = \text{top} + 1$ remains valid.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 2: POP State Transition

**Initial State:** Stack contains $n$ elements, with `top = n - 1`, $n \geq 1$.

**Step 1 — Boundary check (guard against underflow):**

$$
\text{if } (\text{top} = -1) \implies n = 0 \implies \text{STACK IS EMPTY} \implies \text{REJECT}
$$

**Step 2 — Read and store the topmost value:**

$$
\text{ret} = \text{stack}[\text{top}]
$$

**Step 3 — Decrement top pointer:**

$$
\text{top}_{\text{new}} = \text{top}_{\text{old}} - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Update element count:**

$$
n_{\text{new}} = n_{\text{old}} - 1
$$

---

### 🔬 Analytical Trace — Worked Example (MAX = 5)

Trace the operations: `PUSH(10)`, `PUSH(20)`, `PUSH(30)`, `POP()`, `PUSH(40)`, `PEEK()`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial:} \quad & \text{top} = -1,\; \text{stack} = [\_,\_,\_,\_,\_] \\
\text{PUSH(10):} \quad & \text{top} = 0,\; \text{stack} = [10,\_,\_,\_,\_] \\
\text{PUSH(20):} \quad & \text{top} = 1,\; \text{stack} = [10,20,\_,\_,\_] \\
\text{PUSH(30):} \quad & \text{top} = 2,\; \text{stack} = [10,20,30,\_,\_] \\
\text{POP():} \quad & \text{returns 30},\; \text{top} = 1,\; \text{stack} = [10,20,\_,\_,\_] \\
\text{PUSH(40):} \quad & \text{top} = 2,\; \text{stack} = [10,20,40,\_,\_] \\
\text{PEEK():} \quad & \text{returns 40 (no change to top)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 📋 Complete Reference C Program (KTU 2024 Lab Standard)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 5

int stack[MAX];
int top = -1;

void push(int item) {
    if (top == MAX - 1) {
        printf("\nStack Overflow! Cannot push %d.\n", item);
        return;
    }
    top = top + 1;
    stack[top] = item;
    printf("%d pushed successfully.\n", item);
}

int pop() {
    if (top == -1) {
        printf("\nStack Underflow! Stack is empty.\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int item = stack[top];
    top = top - 1;
    printf("%d popped successfully.\n", item);
    return item;
}

int peek() {
    if (top == -1) {
        printf("\nStack is empty.\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return stack[top];
}

void display() {
    if (top == -1) {
        printf("\nStack is empty.\n");
        return;
    }
    printf("\nStack contents (top to bottom):\n");
    for (int i = top; i >= 0; i--) {
        printf("| %d |\n", stack[i]);
    }
}

int main() {
    int choice, item;
    while (1) {
        printf("\n--- Stack Menu ---\n");
        printf("1. PUSH\n2. POP\n3. PEEK\n4. DISPLAY\n5. EXIT\n");
        printf("Enter choice: ");
        scanf("%d", &choice);
        switch (choice) {
            case 1:
                printf("Enter element to push: ");
                scanf("%d", &item);
                push(item);
                break;
            case 2:
                pop();
                break;
            case 3:
                item = peek();
                if (item != -1) printf("Top element: %d\n", item);
                break;
            case 4:
                display();
                break;
            case 5:
                return 0;
            default:
                printf("Invalid choice.\n");
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above program follows the **exact coding pattern** expected in KTU 2024 lab records. Examiners check for: (1) `#define MAX` constant usage, (2) correct `top` initialization to `-1`, (3) overflow/underflow guards, and (4) modular functions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗂️ Diagram 1: Conceptual Stack Architecture (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Stack Array MAX = 5"] --> B["Index 0"]
    A --> C["Index 1"]
    A --> D["Index 2 top = 2"]
    A --> E["Index 3 free"]
    A --> F["Index 4 free"]

    G["top pointer always points here"] -.-> D

    H["PUSH direction: top moves UP"] -.-> D
    I["POP direction: top moves DOWN"] -.-> D
```

---

### 🗂️ Diagram 2: Operational State Machine for Stack Operations

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> PartiallyFilled : push x
    PartiallyFilled --> Full : push x repeatedly
    Full --> PartiallyFilled : pop
    PartiallyFilled --> Empty : pop until empty
    Full --> Full : push x OVERFLOW rejected
    Empty --> Empty : pop UNDERFLOW rejected
```

> **State Definitions:**
> - **Empty:** `top = -1`, zero elements present.
> - **Partially Filled:** `0 <= top <= MAX - 2`, between 1 and MAX-1 elements.
> - **Full:** `top = MAX - 1`, exactly MAX elements present.

---

### 🗂️ Diagram 3: Function Call Flow for PUSH Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: push item"] --> B{"top == MAX - 1 ?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Print Overflow Message"]
    C --> Z["Return to Caller"]
    B -- "No" --> D["top = top + 1"]
    D --> E["stack top = item"]
    E --> F["Print Success Message"]
    F --> Z["Return to Caller"]
```

---

### 🗂️ Diagram 4: Memory Layout of Stack Array

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["stack 0"] -->|"10"| B["stack 1"]
    B -->|"20"| C["stack 2"]
    C -->|"40"| D["stack 3"]
    D -->|"empty"| E["stack 4"]
    F["top = 2"] -.-> C
    style A fill:#d4f4dd
    style B fill:#d4f4dd
    style C fill:#ffe599
    style D fill:#f4cccc
    style E fill:#f4cccc
```

> **Color Legend:**
> - 🟢 **Green slots** — Valid stored elements.
> - 🟡 **Yellow slot** — The current top element.
> - 🔴 **Red slots** — Free / unoccupied positions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
*Define a stack data structure. What do you mean by overflow and underflow conditions in a stack implemented using an array?*

**Model Answer:**

A **stack** is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** principle, supporting insertion (PUSH) and deletion (POP) at one end called the **top**.

- **Overflow:** Occurs when `top == MAX - 1`; the stack is full and a new element cannot be inserted. The PUSH operation must be rejected.
- **Underflow:** Occurs when `top == -1`; the stack is empty and a POP operation cannot return a valid element. The POP operation must be rejected.

Both conditions must be checked **before** modifying the `top` pointer to maintain stack integrity. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**  
*What is the time complexity of PUSH and POP operations in a stack implemented using an array? Justify.*

**Model Answer:**

Both **PUSH** and **POP** operations in an array-based stack have a time complexity of **O(1)**, i.e., **constant time**.

**Justification:** Each operation involves:
1. A single comparison (`top == MAX - 1` or `top == -1`) — **O(1)**.
2. A single increment/decrement of `top` — **O(1)**.
3. A single array assignment or read — **O(1)**.

No loops, no traversal, and no dependent steps exist. Hence, irrespective of the stack size, both operations take the same fixed amount of time. **(3 marks)**

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Stack Implementation and Trace

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** Write a C program to implement a stack using an array. Your program should support the operations PUSH, POP, PEEK, and DISPLAY, with proper overflow and underflow checks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given an integer stack with `MAX = 6` and starting from an empty state, perform the following sequence of operations and display the stack contents after each operation: `PUSH(5), PUSH(15), PUSH(25), POP, PUSH(35), PUSH(45), PUSH(55), POP, PUSH(65)`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) C Program Implementation (7 marks)**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 100

int stack[MAX];
int top = -1;

void push(int x) {
    if (top == MAX - 1) {
        printf("Stack Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    top++;
    stack[top] = x;
    printf("%d pushed\n", x);
}

int pop() {
    if (top == -1) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    int x = stack[top];
    top--;
    return x;
}

int peek() {
    if (top == -1) {
        printf("Stack is empty\n");
        return -1;
    }
    return stack[top];
}

void display() {
    if (top == -1) {
        printf("Stack is empty\n");
        return;
    }
    printf("Stack (top to bottom): ");
    for (int i = top; i >= 0; i--) {
        printf("%d ", stack[i]);
    }
    printf("\n");
}

int main() {
    int ch, val;
    while (1) {
        printf("\n1:Push 2:Pop 3:Peek 4:Display 5:Exit\n");
        scanf("%d", &ch);
        if (ch == 5) break;
        switch (ch) {
            case 1: scanf("%d", &val); push(val); break;
            case 2: val = pop();
                    if (val != -1) printf("Popped: %d\n", val);
                    break;
            case 3: val = peek();
                    if (val != -1) printf("Top: %d\n", val);
                    break;
            case 4: display(); break;
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**
- Correct `#define`, `top = -1` initialization: **1 mark**
- Overflow check in PUSH: **1 mark**
- Underflow check in POP: **1 mark**
- Correct increment/decrement logic: **2 marks**
- PEEK and DISPLAY functions: **2 marks**

---

**(b) Step-by-Step Trace (7 marks)**

Given `MAX = 6`, initial state: `top = -1`, stack empty.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{PUSH(5)}: && \text{top} = 0, && \text{stack} = [5, -, -, -, -, -] \\
& \text{PUSH(15)}: && \text{top} = 1, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, -, -, -, -] \\
& \text{PUSH(25)}: && \text{top} = 2, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, 25, -, -, -] \\
& \text{POP()}: && \text{top} = 1, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, -, -, -, -] \\
& \text{PUSH(35)}: && \text{top} = 2, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, 35, -, -, -] \\
& \text{PUSH(45)}: && \text{top} = 3, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, 35, 45, -, -] \\
& \text{PUSH(55)}: && \text{top} = 4, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, 35, 45, 55, -] \\
& \text{PUSH}(65): && \text{top} = 5, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, 35, 45, 55, 65] \\
& \text{POP()}: && \text{top} = 4, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, 35, 45, 55, -] \\
& \text{PUSH}(65): && \text{top} = 5, && \text{stack} = [5, 15, 35, 45, 55, 65]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update `top` after POP** — common, costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Incorrect initial value of `top`** — must be `-1`, not `0`. (0 mark if wrong).
> 3. **Confusing overflow check** — must compare `top == MAX - 1`, not `top == MAX`.
> 4. **Skipping the underflow guard in POP** — board examiners deduct 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Writing `top++` *before* the overflow check** — the guard must come **first**.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a stack and list any **four real-world applications** of stacks. Explain how stacks are used in **function call management** by the operating system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **array-based stack implementation** with **linked-list-based stack implementation** with respect to memory usage, overflow handling, and access time. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Stack Concept and Applications (7 marks)**

A **stack** is a non-primitive, linear data structure based on the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** discipline. The end at which both insertion and deletion occur is called the **top**.

**Four Real-World Applications:**
1. **Undo/Redo in editors** — Each user action is PUSHed onto the stack; Undo POPS it.
2. **Browser back button** — Visited URLs are PUSHed; back button POPs.
3. **Compiler recursion** — Recursive function calls are tracked using the **call stack**.
4. **Expression evaluation** — Infix expressions are converted to postfix using a stack.
5. (Bonus) **Backtracking algorithms** like maze-solving and depth-first search.

**Function Call Management:**
When a function is called, the system creates a **stack frame** (also called an **activation record**) containing:
- Return address (where to resume after the function ends)
- Local variables
- Function arguments

This frame is **PUSHed** onto the **call stack**. When the function completes, its frame is **POPped**, and execution resumes at the saved return address. This mechanism enables **nested function calls** and **recursion**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Array vs Linked-List Stack Comparison (7 marks)**

| Comparison Parameter | Array-Based Stack | Linked-List-Based Stack |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory allocation** | Static (compile-time, fixed `MAX`) | Dynamic (runtime, grows on demand) |
| **Overflow handling** | Possible — array has a fixed upper limit | Not possible in theory — limited only by system memory |
| **Access time** | **O(1)** — direct index access | **O(1)** — pointer access from head |
| **Memory overhead** | No extra per-node overhead | Extra pointer storage per node |
| **Cache friendliness** | High — contiguous memory | Low — nodes scattered in heap |
| **Implementation complexity** | Simpler | More complex (pointer manipulation) |

**Array Stack — Advantage:** Simple, cache-efficient, no pointer overhead.  
**Array Stack — Disadvantage:** Fixed size, wastage if underutilized; overflow possible.

**Linked-List Stack — Advantage:** Dynamic size, no overflow (until memory exhausted).  
**Linked-List Stack — Disadvantage:** Extra memory for pointers, risk of memory leaks. **(7 marks)**

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Stack = LIFO** linear data structure.
- ✅ **`top` is initialized to `-1`** to represent an empty stack.
- ✅ **Overflow** check: `top == MAX - 1` → before PUSH.
- ✅ **Underflow** check: `top == -1` → before POP.
- ✅ **PUSH:** `top++; stack[top] = item;`
- ✅ **POP:** `item = stack[top]; top--;`
- ✅ **Time complexity** of all primary operations: **O(1)**.
- ✅ **Space complexity** of primary operations: **O(1)**; overall stack: **O(n)** where $n$ is the number of elements.
- ✅ Element count relationship: $n = \text{top} + 1$.
- ✅ Array stack = **fixed size, no dynamic growth**, simpler than linked-list version.
- ✅ Applications: **call stack, undo/redo, expression evaluation, DFS, backtracking**.
- ✅ **PEEK** does not modify `top`; POP does.
- ✅ In KTU exams, **always check boundary conditions first**, then modify `top`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utilization of stack to convert infix expression to postfix expression and evaluate postfix expression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL308 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Utilization of stack to convert infix expression to postfix expression and evaluate postfix expression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **arithmetic expression** is a syntactically correct combination of operands, operators, and parentheses that represents a computational value. Based on the relative positioning of operators and operands, expressions are classified into three principal notations:

| Notation | Structure | Example | Common Name |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Infix | Operator placed **between** two operands | `A + B` | Standard mathematical form |
| Prefix (Polish) | Operator placed **before** its operands | `+ A B` | Used in compiler parsing, LISP |
| Postfix (Reverse Polish) | Operator placed **after** its operands | `A B +` | Used in stack machines, HP calculators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** A stack is the **canonical and mandatory** data structure for converting an infix expression to its equivalent postfix form and for evaluating a postfix expression. This experiment is a direct application of the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** property of stacks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Railway Shunting Yard Analogy (Alfred Aho, Jeff Ullman — the classic "Shunting Yard Algorithm" of 1973)**
>
> Imagine a railway shunting yard where train cars (operands) arrive on a single track and operators (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`) act as **switches that re-route cars**. The output track is where the **postfix train** is assembled. A **holding track** (the stack) temporarily parks operators whose precedence is *not yet* satisfied. When an operator of *lower* priority arrives, the higher-priority operators in the holding track are "shunted out" (popped) to the output first. This is precisely the intuition behind Dijkstra's shunting-yard algorithm.

For **postfix evaluation**, the analogy is even simpler: imagine a **reverse-Polish calculator** (the original HP-12C financial calculator used this!). You push every number you see onto a stack; when you see an operator, you pop the **top two** numbers, apply the operator, and push the result back. The final value on the stack is the answer.

## 1.3 Why Postfix? (The Engineering "Why")

- **No parentheses required:** Operator precedence is encoded *implicitly* in the ordering. A compiler does not need to scan backward.
- **Single left-to-right pass:** Both conversion and evaluation are $O(n)$ — linear in the length of the expression.
- **Direct machine execution:** Postfix maps cleanly to a **stack-based virtual machine** (JVM bytecode uses a related concept; Forth and PostScript are pure postfix languages).
- **No precedence table lookup at runtime:** Precedence is resolved *once* during conversion.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** In this experiment, we restrict the infix expression to **single-letter/digit operands** and the binary operators `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`, along with parentheses `(`, `)`.
> The assumption is that the input infix expression is **syntactically valid** (no malformed parentheses, no unary operators).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operator Precedence & Associativity (The "Rules of the Game")

Every operator carries two attributes that govern the order of evaluation:

1. **Precedence** — *Which operator binds tighter?*
2. **Associativity** — *When two operators of equal precedence meet, which one evaluates first?*

### 2.1.1 The Standard KTU / C-Language Precedence Table

| Operator | Precedence Level | Associativity | Stack Behaviour on Arrival |
|----------|:----------------:|:-------------:|----------------------------|
| `^` (exponentiation) | 3 (Highest) | Right-to-Left $\rightarrow$ | Pop while stack-top has **higher or equal** precedence |
| `*` , `/` (multiplicative) | 2 | Left-to-Right $\leftarrow$ | Pop while stack-top has **higher or equal** precedence |
| `+` , `-` (additive) | 1 (Lowest) | Left-to-Right $\leftarrow$ | Pop while stack-top has **higher or equal** precedence |
| `(` (left parenthesis) | Special | N/A | **Always** push; never pop until matching `)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Distinction for `^`:** Because `^` is right-associative, the on-arrival rule for the **incoming** `^` is: *pop operators of strictly higher precedence only — do NOT pop an existing `^` from the stack top*. This subtle difference is a favourite KTU viva question and a common coding bug.

## 2.2 Algorithm A: Infix $\rightarrow$ Postfix Conversion (Shunting Yard)

**Input:** An infix expression string `E` of length $n$.
**Output:** Equivalent postfix expression string `P`.
**Data Structure:** A character stack `S`.

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic (Token-by-Token)

For each token `t` scanned left-to-right from `E`:

| Token Type | Action |
|------------|--------|
| **Operand** (`a-z`, `0-9`) | Append `t` to output `P` directly. |
| **Left Parenthesis** `(` | Push `(` onto stack `S`. |
| **Right Parenthesis** `)` | Repeatedly pop and append the stack-top to `P` until `(` is popped. **Discard** the `(` itself. |
| **Operator** `op` | While `S` is not empty AND `top(S)` is an operator AND `precedence(top(S))` $\geq$ `precedence(op)`: pop and append. **Special case:** if `op` is `^`, use strict `>` only (right-associative). Then push `op` onto `S`. |
| **End of input** | Pop and append every remaining operator from `S` to `P`. |

### 2.2.2 Pseudocode

```
function infixToPostfix(E):
    initialize empty stack S
    initialize empty string P
    for each token t in E (left to right):
        if t is operand:
            P ← P + t
        else if t is '(':
            S.push(t)
        else if t is ')':
            while S.top() ≠ '(':
                P ← P + S.pop()
            S.pop()            // discard the '('
        else if t is operator:
            while (not S.isEmpty()) 
                  AND (S.top() is operator) 
                  AND ((precedence(S.top()) > precedence(t)) 
                       OR (precedence(S.top()) == precedence(t) AND t ≠ '^')):
                P ← P + S.pop()
            S.push(t)
    while not S.isEmpty():
        P ← P + S.pop()
    return P
```

## 2.3 Algorithm B: Postfix Expression Evaluation

**Input:** A postfix expression string `Q` of length $m$ consisting of single-digit integers and binary operators.
**Output:** A single integer result.
**Data Structure:** A numeric (integer) stack `S`.

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

For each token `t` scanned left-to-right from `Q`:

| Token Type | Action |
|------------|--------|
| **Operand** | Convert to numeric value `v`; `S.push(v)`. |
| **Operator** `op` | Pop the **top two** values: let `a = S.pop()` (right operand) and `b = S.pop()` (left operand). Compute `r = b op a`. Push `r` onto `S`. |
| **End of input** | The final value `S.pop()` is the result. (For valid input, the stack will contain exactly one element.) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Operand Order is Critical!** The first pop is the **right operand** and the second pop is the **left operand**. Reversing them produces the wrong answer for non-commutative operators (`-`, `/`, `^`). This is the #1 cause of evaluation bugs.

### 2.3.2 Pseudocode

```
function evaluatePostfix(Q):
    initialize empty stack S
    for each token t in Q (left to right):
        if t is digit:
            S.push( (int) t )
        else if t is operator:
            a ← S.pop()                  // right operand
            b ← S.pop()                  // left operand
            S.push( apply(b, t, a) )
    return S.pop()
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Equation | Used In |
|---------|-----------------|---------|
| Total stack pushes for conversion | $\leq n$ | Complexity |
| Total stack pushes for evaluation | $\leq m$ | Complexity |
| Time complexity of conversion | $T(n) = O(n)$ | Each token processed once |
| Time complexity of evaluation | $T(m) = O(m)$ | Each token processed once |
| Space complexity (worst case) | $S(n) = O(n)$ | All operands in `(((a)))` |
| Associativity rule for `^` | Pop on `>` only | Right-associative |
| Associativity rule for `+ - * /` | Pop on `$\geq$` | Left-associative |
| Pop count on `)` | Equals number of operators between matching `(` `)` | Postfix construction |
| Final stack size (valid postfix) | $= 1$ | Evaluation check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Operator Precedence as a Function:** Formally, define $\text{prec}(op) \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ as above, and $\text{assoc}(op) \in \{L, R\}$. The pop-condition for an incoming operator $op_i$ with stack-top $op_t$ is:
> $$\text{pop } op_t \iff \big(\text{prec}(op_t) > \text{prec}(op_i)\big) \;\lor\; \big(\text{prec}(op_t) = \text{prec}(op_i) \;\land\; \text{assoc}(op_i) = L\big)$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compilers (gcc, javac):** The front-end parser uses a shunting-yard pass to convert infix source code to an **AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)** or directly to postfix **three-address code**.
- **HP-12C / HP-48 Financial Calculators:** All inputs are evaluated in postfix (Reverse Polish Notation, RPN).
- **PostScript / Forth / Joy:** Entire programming languages are built on postfix evaluation.
- **JVM Bytecode:** The `iadd`, `isub`, `imul`, `idiv` opcodes operate on an **operand stack**, mirroring postfix evaluation.
- **Excel Formula Internals:** Excel first converts `=A1+B2*C3` to a postfix form before evaluation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Infix to Postfix Conversion

**Convert:** $\;E = \mathbf{a + b * c \;\;-\;\; (d / e + f) * g}$

**Precedence (highest to lowest):** `^` > `*`, `/` > `+`, `-` (all left-associative except `^`).

### 3.1.1 Exhaustive Token-by-Token Trace

We maintain a Stack `S` (drawn vertically, top on the right) and an Output `P`.

| Step | Token | Action (Algorithm) | Stack (bottom $\rightarrow$ top) | Output `P` |
|:----:|:-----:|--------------------|----------------------------------|------------|
| 0 | — | Initial state | *(empty)* | *(empty)* |
| 1 | `a` | Operand $\rightarrow$ append to `P` | *(empty)* | `a` |
| 2 | `+` | Stack empty $\rightarrow$ push | `+` | `a` |
| 3 | `b` | Operand $\rightarrow$ append to `P` | `+` | `a b` |
| 4 | `*` | Top is `+` (prec $1$); $\text{prec}(*) = 2 > 1$; no pop. Push `*`. | `+ *` | `a b` |
| 5 | `c` | Operand $\rightarrow$ append to `P` | `+ *` | `a b c` |
| 6 | `-` | Top is `*` (prec $2$); $\text{prec}(-) = 1 < 2$ $\Rightarrow$ pop `*`, append. Top now `+` (prec $1$); $1 \geq 1$ $\Rightarrow$ pop `+`, append. Stack empty. Push `-`. | `-` | `a b c * +` |
| 7 | `(` | Left paren $\rightarrow$ always push | `- (` | `a b c * +` |
| 8 | `d` | Operand $\rightarrow$ append | `- (` | `a b c * + d` |
| 9 | `/` | Top is `(` $\rightarrow$ no pop. Push `/`. | `- ( /` | `a b c * + d` |
| 10 | `e` | Operand $\rightarrow$ append | `- ( /` | `a b c * + d e` |
| 11 | `+` | Top is `/` (prec $2$); $2 \geq 1$ $\Rightarrow$ pop `/`, append. Top is `(` $\rightarrow$ stop. Push `+`. | `- ( +` | `a b c * + d e /` |
| 12 | `f` | Operand $\rightarrow$ append | `- ( +` | `a b c * + d e / f` |
| 13 | `)` | Pop until `(`: pop `+`, append. Discard `(`. | `-` | `a b c * + d e / f +` |
| 14 | `*` | Top is `-` (prec $1$); $\text{prec}(*) = 2 > 1$; no pop. Push `*`. | `- *` | `a b c * + d e / f +` |
| 15 | `g` | Operand $\rightarrow$ append | `- *` | `a b c * + d e / f + g` |
| 16 | end | Pop all: pop `*`, append; pop `-`, append. | *(empty)* | `a b c * + d e / f + g * -` |

### 3.1.2 Final Result

$$\boxed{\;P \;=\; a\; b\; c\; *\; +\; d\; e\; /\; f\; +\; g\; *\; -\;}$$

### 3.1.3 Verification (Manual)

Reading `a b c * + d e / f + g * -` left-to-right in postfix evaluation order:
- `b c *` $\Rightarrow$ $b \cdot c$
- `a (b c *) +` $\Rightarrow$ $a + bc$
- `d e /` $\Rightarrow$ $d / e$
- `(d/e) f +` $\Rightarrow$ $d/e + f$
- `((d/e)+f) g *` $\Rightarrow$ $(d/e + f) \cdot g$
- `(a+bc) ((d/e+f)g) -` $\Rightarrow$ $a + bc - (d/e+f) \cdot g$ ✓

This matches the original infix expression.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Postfix Expression Evaluation

**Evaluate:** $\;Q = \mathbf{5 \;\; 6 \;\; 2 \;\; + \;\; * \;\; 12 \;\; 4 \;\; / \;\; -}$

**Step-by-step numeric stack trace:**

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (bottom $\rightarrow$ top) | Notes |
|:----:|:-----:|--------|----------------------------------|-------|
| 0 | — | Initial | *(empty)* | — |
| 1 | `5` | Push 5 | `5` | — |
| 2 | `6` | Push 6 | `5 6` | — |
| 3 | `2` | Push 2 | `5 6 2` | — |
| 4 | `+` | Pop 2 (right), pop 6 (left); $6+2=8$; push 8 | `5 8` | $b=6, a=2$ |
| 5 | `*` | Pop 8 (right), pop 5 (left); $5 \cdot 8 = 40$; push 40 | `40` | $b=5, a=8$ |
| 6 | `12` | Push 12 | `40 12` | — |
| 7 | `4` | Push 4 | `40 12 4` | — |
| 8 | `/` | Pop 4 (right), pop 12 (left); $12/4=3$; push 3 | `40 3` | $b=12, a=4$ |
| 9 | `-` | Pop 3 (right), pop 40 (left); $40-3=37$; push 37 | `37` | $b=40, a=3$ |
| 10 | end | Pop final $\Rightarrow$ result | *(empty)* | Result = **37** |

### 3.2.1 Final Result

$$\boxed{\text{Result} = 37}$$

### 3.2.2 Analytical Verification

Decompose the postfix into its evaluation tree:
- Subexpression $A = 6 + 2 = 8$
- Subexpression $B = 5 \cdot A = 5 \cdot 8 = 40$
- Subexpression $C = 12 / 4 = 3$
- Final $= B - C = 40 - 3 = 37$ ✓

This corresponds to the original infix: $(5 \cdot (6+2)) - (12/4) = 40 - 3 = 37$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — The `^` (Right-Associative) Edge Case

**Convert:** $\;E = \mathbf{a \;\; \mathbf{\hat{}} \;\; b \;\; \mathbf{\hat{}} \;\; c}$

Without the right-associative rule, the conversion would be incorrect.

| Step | Token | Action | Stack | Output |
|:----:|:-----:|--------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `a` | Append | *(empty)* | `a` |
| 2 | `^` | Stack empty $\rightarrow$ push | `^` | `a` |
| 3 | `b` | Append | `^` | `a b` |
| 4 | `^` | Top is `^` (prec $3$); $\text{prec}(^\text{incoming})=3$; since associativity of incoming is Right $\Rightarrow$ do **NOT** pop. Push. | `^ ^` | `a b` |
| 5 | end | Pop both `^`s | *(empty)* | `a b ^ ^` |

**Result:** $a\; b\; \mathbf{\hat{}}\; \mathbf{\hat{}}\;$ which correctly evaluates to $a^{(b^c)}$ — the right-associative interpretation.

> [!WARNING]
> If you had used the $\geq$ rule (left-associative) for `^`, the output would be `a b ^ c ^` = $(a^b)^c$ — **mathematically wrong** for exponentiation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Infix-to-Postfix Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize empty stack S and output string P]
    B --> C{More tokens in infix expression E?}
    C -- No --> Z[Pop all remaining operators from S to P]
    Z --> Y([Return P as postfix expression])
    C -- Yes --> D[Read next token t]
    D --> E{t is an operand?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Append t to P]
    F --> C
    E -- No --> G{t is a left parenthesis ?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Push t onto S]
    H --> C
    G -- No --> I{t is a right parenthesis ?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Pop from S and append to P until top is left parenthesis]
    J --> K[Pop and discard the left parenthesis]
    K --> C
    I -- No --> L{t is an operator?}
    L -- Yes --> M[While S is not empty AND top of S is an operator AND precedence condition holds]
    M --> N[Pop top of S and append to P]
    N --> O{Precedence condition still holds?}
    O -- Yes --> M
    O -- No --> P[Push t onto S]
    P --> C
    L -- No --> Q[Skip invalid token]
    Q --> C
```

## 4.2 Postfix Evaluation Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize empty numeric stack S]
    B --> C{More tokens in postfix expression Q?}
    C -- No --> R[Pop final result from S]
    R --> S([Return result as evaluated value])
    C -- Yes --> D[Read next token t]
    D --> E{t is a digit or operand?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Convert t to numeric value v]
    F --> G[Push v onto S]
    G --> C
    E -- No --> H{t is an operator?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Pop a from S as right operand]
    I --> J[Pop b from S as left operand]
    J --> K[Compute result r = b operator a]
    K --> L[Push r onto S]
    L --> C
    H -- No --> M[Invalid token - report error]
    M --> C
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Input Infix Expression] --> B[Tokenizer Module]
    B --> C{Infix to Postfix Engine}
    C --> D[Operator Precedence Module]
    C --> E[Character Stack S]
    C --> F[Postfix Output String]
    F --> G{Postfix Evaluator Engine}
    G --> H[Arithmetic Module]
    G --> I[Numeric Stack N]
    G --> J[Final Result]
    D -.uses.-> K[Precedence Lookup Table]
    H -.uses.-> L[Operator Function Set]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** [3 Marks]
> **Q:** *Differentiate between infix, prefix, and postfix notations. Give one example of each using the expression $(x+y) \cdot z$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Infix notation:** Operator is placed *between* the operands. It is the standard mathematical form, but requires precedence rules and parentheses for unambiguous evaluation.  
  **Example:** $(x+y) \cdot z$
- **Prefix (Polish) notation:** Operator is placed *before* its operands. It is parenthesization-free and is evaluated right-to-left.  
  **Example:** $\cdot + x y z$ (i.e., $\cdot (+ \; x \; y) \; z$)
- **Postfix (Reverse Polish) notation:** Operator is placed *after* its operands. It is parenthesization-free and is evaluated left-to-right using a stack.  
  **Example:** $x\; y\; + \; z\; \cdot$

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** [3 Marks]
> **Q:** *Why is a stack the natural data structure for postfix evaluation? Explain with the order of operand popping.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A stack is the natural data structure because postfix evaluation requires that whenever an operator is encountered, its **two operands must be the most recently produced (but not yet used) values** — this is exactly the LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) property of a stack.

The popping order is critical:
1. Pop the top element and treat it as the **right operand** (`a`).
2. Pop the next element and treat it as the **left operand** (`b`).
3. Apply the operator: $\text{result} = b \; op \; a$.
4. Push the result back onto the stack.

Reversing the order of popping produces incorrect results for non-commutative operators like subtraction and division.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Pattern]

> **(a)** Convert the following infix expression to postfix form using a stack. Show the **complete stack trace** for every token. [7 Marks]
> $$E = \mathbf{\;(\;A + B\;) \;\; \mathbf{\hat{}} \;\; C \;\; - \;\; D \;\; / \;\; (\;E - F\;\)}$$

> **(b)** Evaluate the following postfix expression using a stack. Show the **complete numeric stack trace** for every token. State the result. [7 Marks]
> $$Q = \mathbf{\;7 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; * \;\; - \;\; 4 \;\; + \;\; 9 \;\; 8 \;\; / \;\; +}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Precedence:** `^` (3, R) > `*`, `/` (2, L) > `+`, `-` (1, L). Parentheses force evaluation order.

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (bottom $\rightarrow$ top) | Output `P` |
|:----:|:-----:|--------|----------------------------------|------------|
| 1 | `(` | Push `(` | `(` | — |
| 2 | `A` | Append | `(` | `A` |
| 3 | `+` | Top is `(` $\rightarrow$ no pop. Push `+`. | `( +` | `A` |
| 4 | `B` | Append | `( +` | `A B` |
| 5 | `)` | Pop `+` to `P`; pop & discard `(`. | *(empty)* | `A B +` |
| 6 | `^` | Stack empty $\rightarrow$ push. | `^` | `A B +` |
| 7 | `C` | Append | `^` | `A B + C` |
| 8 | `-` | Top is `^` (prec 3); $\text{prec}(-)=1 < 3$ $\Rightarrow$ pop `^`, append. Stack empty. Push `-`. | `-` | `A B + C ^` |
| 9 | `D` | Append | `-` | `A B + C ^ D` |
| 10 | `/` | Top is `-` (prec 1); $\text{prec}(/)=2 > 1$; no pop. Push `/`. | `- /` | `A B + C ^ D` |
| 11 | `(` | Push `(`. | `- / (` | `A B + C ^ D` |
| 12 | `E` | Append | `- / (` | `A B + C ^ D E` |
| 13 | `-` | Top is `(` $\rightarrow$ no pop. Push `-`. | `- / ( -` | `A B + C ^ D E` |
| 14 | `F` | Append | `- / ( -` | `A B + C ^ D E F` |
| 15 | `)` | Pop `-` to `P`; pop & discard `(`. | `- /` | `A B + C ^ D E F -` |
| 16 | end | Pop `/`; pop `-`. | *(empty)* | `A B + C ^ D E F - / -` |

**Final postfix expression:**

$$\boxed{P = A\; B\; + \; C\; \mathbf{\hat{}}\; D\; E\; F\; -\; /\; -}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Evaluate $Q = 7\; 2\; 3\; *\; - \;4\; + \;9\; 8\; / \;+$

| Step | Token | Action | Stack (bottom $\rightarrow$ top) |
|:----:|:-----:|--------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | `7` | Push 7 | `7` |
| 2 | `2` | Push 2 | `7 2` |
| 3 | `3` | Push 3 | `7 2 3` |
| 4 | `*` | Pop $a=3, b=2$; $r=2\cdot 3=6$; push 6 | `7 6` |
| 5 | `-` | Pop $a=6, b=7$; $r=7-6=1$; push 1 | `1` |
| 6 | `4` | Push 4 | `1 4` |
| 7 | `+` | Pop $a=4, b=1$; $r=1+4=5$; push 5 | `5` |
| 8 | `9` | Push 9 | `5 9` |
| 9 | `8` | Push 8 | `5 9 8` |
| 10 | `/` | Pop $a=8, b=9$; $r=9/8=1$ (integer); push 1 | `5 1` |
| 11 | `+` | Pop $a=1, b=5$; $r=5+1=6$; push 6 | `6` |
| 12 | end | Pop final | — |

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\text{Result} = 6}$$

#### Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative)

> **(a)** Convert $E = \mathbf{\;P - Q \;\; / \;\; R + S \; \mathbf{\hat{}}\; T\;}$ to postfix showing full trace. [7 Marks]

> **(b)** Evaluate $Q = \mathbf{\;6 \;\; 5 \;\; 2 \;\; \mathbf{\hat{}}\;\; / \;\; 3 \;\; * \;\; 4 \;\; +}$ showing full trace. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution (a):**

| Step | Token | Action | Stack | Output |
|:----:|:-----:|--------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `P` | Append | *(empty)* | `P` |
| 2 | `-` | Push | `-` | `P` |
| 3 | `Q` | Append | `-` | `P Q` |
| 4 | `/` | Top `-` (prec 1); $\text{prec}(/)=2 > 1$; no pop. Push. | `- /` | `P Q` |
| 5 | `R` | Append | `- /` | `P Q R` |
| 6 | `+` | Top `/` (prec 2); $\text{prec}(+)=1 < 2$ $\Rightarrow$ pop `/`. Top `-` (prec 1); $1 \geq 1$ $\Rightarrow$ pop `-`. Push `+`. | `+` | `P Q R / -` |
| 7 | `S` | Append | `+` | `P Q R / - S` |
| 8 | `^` | Top `+` (prec 1); $3 > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ pop `+`. Push `^`. | `^` | `P Q R / - S +` |
| 9 | `T` | Append | `^` | `P Q R / - S + T` |
| 10 | end | Pop `^` | *(empty)* | `P Q R / - S + T ^` |

**Result:** $\boxed{P\; Q\; R\; /\; - \;S\; + \;T\; \mathbf{\hat{}}}$

**Model Solution (b):**

| Step | Token | Action | Stack |
|:----:|:-----:|--------|-------|
| 1 | `6` | Push | `6` |
| 2 | `5` | Push | `6 5` |
| 3 | `2` | Push | `6 5 2` |
| 4 | `^` | $5^2 = 25$; push | `6 25` |
| 5 | `/` | $6/25 = 0$ (int); push | `0` |
| 6 | `3` | Push | `0 3` |
| 7 | `*` | $0 \cdot 3 = 0$; push | `0` |
| 8 | `4` | Push | `0 4` |
| 9 | `+` | $0 + 4 = 4$; push | `4` |

**Result:** $\boxed{4}$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Experiment (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Wrong operand order in evaluation** (-2 to -3 marks): Students write `a op b` instead of `b op a` when popping, producing wrong results for `-`, `/`, `^`.
> 2. **Forgetting the right-associative rule for `^`** (-1 to -2 marks): Using `$\geq$` everywhere gives $(a^b)^c$ instead of $a^{(b^c)}$.
> 3. **Not discarding `(`** (-1 mark): When `)` is encountered, the matching `(` is popped but should be **discarded** — it does not appear in postfix output.
> 4. **Empty stack access** (-1 mark / runtime error): Failing to check `isEmpty()` before `pop()` or `peek()`.
> 5. **Missing the final drain step** (-2 marks): Operators still on the stack at end-of-input must be popped and appended; skipping this leaves the postfix incomplete.
> 6. **Inconsistent precedence coding** (-1 to -2 marks): Hard-coding precedence via if-else chains without a clean lookup function leads to bugs and is hard to debug in the lab.
> 7. **Division by zero** in evaluation: Not the algorithm's fault, but KTU examiners may test it; always check the right operand for `/`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Infix:** Operator *between* operands — human-friendly, precedence-dependent.
- **Postfix:** Operator *after* operands — machine-friendly, precedence-implicit.
- **Stack LIFO property** is the cornerstone of both algorithms.
- **Three rules for token handling in conversion:**
  1. Operand $\rightarrow$ output.
  2. `(` $\rightarrow$ push unconditionally.
  3. `)` $\rightarrow$ pop until matching `(` is found and discarded.
- **Operator handling:** Pop from stack while the top has higher (or equal, for left-assoc) precedence; then push the new operator.
- **Right-associativity of `^`** requires **strict `>`** for popping, not `$\geq$`.
- **Final drain step** is mandatory — pop everything remaining in the stack.
- **Evaluation order:** First pop = right operand, second pop = left operand.
- **Both algorithms are $O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ space** — linear and optimal.
- **Final stack size = 1** is a built-in integrity check for a valid postfix expression.
- **KTU viva favourites:**
  - "Why not use a queue?"
  - "What happens if the input is `((((a))))`?"  *(Answer: lots of pushes, all discarded, postfix = `a`)*
  - "How do you handle multi-digit numbers?"  *(Answer: extend the tokenizer; the algorithm is unchanged.)*
  - "What is the difference between prefix and postfix conversion?"  *(Answer: scan direction and output order reversed; `^` associativity rule preserved.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of polynomials using arrays and perform polynomial addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL308 - DATA STRUCTURES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Representation of polynomials using arrays and perform polynomial addition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **polynomial** is a mathematical expression consisting of variables (indeterminates) and coefficients, involving only the operations of addition, subtraction, multiplication, and non-negative integer exponents of variables. In the context of data structures, a polynomial of a single variable $x$ with degree $n$ is formally expressed as: $$P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + a_{n-2} x^{n-2} + \dots + a_1 x + a_0$$ where $a_n, a_{n-1}, \dots, a_0$ are the **coefficients** and $n$ is the **degree** of the polynomial, with $a_n \neq 0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A polynomial $P(x)$ of degree $n$ can be compactly represented in memory using a one-dimensional array where the **array index stores the exponent** (power of $x$) and the **array element stores the coefficient** of that corresponding term. This is called the *sparse-friendly dense array representation*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a polynomial like a **ladder in a parking garage**: - Each **rung (step)** of the ladder represents a power of $x$ (like floor number). - The **height of each rung** represents how strong that term is (the coefficient). - Floor **0** is the ground floor (constant term $a_0$). - Floor **3** is the third floor (cubic term $a_3 x^3$). - If a floor is missing, it just has a height of **0** — it exists structurally but contributes nothing. Using an **array** to store a polynomial is like building that ladder in code. We use an array of size `degree + 1`, and `arr[i]` stores the coefficient of $x^i$. The *index is the exponent*, and the *value is the coefficient*. This makes addition of polynomials as simple as **adding corresponding array elements** — just like stacking the rungs of two ladders side-by-side and taking the taller one at each floor. ### 1.3 Why Array Representation? > [!NOTE] > **Why not just use a linked list?** For **dense polynomials** (where most coefficients are non-zero), array representation is preferred because: > 1. **O(1) random access** to any coefficient via index. > 2. **Better cache locality** — contiguous memory. > 3. **Simpler polynomial addition** — single loop over degree. > 4. **No extra memory overhead** for pointers. For **sparse polynomials** (very few non-zero terms among many zero terms), a linked list is more memory-efficient. KTU Module 2 uses the **array representation** for simplicity and speed. ### 1.4 Types of Polynomial Representation (KTU Focus) | Representation | Best For | Memory Pattern | |---|---|---| | **Dense Array** | Polynomials with most non-zero terms | $O(n)$ where $n$ = degree | | **Sparse Array (with term list)** | Polynomials with few non-zero terms | $O(k)$ where $k$ = number of non-zero terms | | **Linked List of Terms** | Sparse + dynamic degree | $O(k)$ with pointer overhead | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 2 Standard:** In this experiment, the polynomial is stored as a **sparse-friendly array of structs** where each element contains only the non-zero terms along with their exponents. The structure `struct poly { int coeff; int expo; }` is used. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Data Structure Design The standard KTU representation uses an array of structures to hold **only non-zero terms**, with a global counter to track the current number of terms: $$\text{Term}_i = \{ \text{coeff}_i, \text{expo}_i \}$$ The full structure is: $$\text{PolyArray} = [\{\text{coeff}_0, \text{expo}_0\}, \{\text{coeff}_1, \text{expo}_1\}, \dots, \{\text{coeff}_{k-1}, \text{expo}_{k-1}\}]$$ where $k$ is the actual count of non-zero terms. ### 2.2 Why This Representation Works — The 'Why' and 'How' **Why store only non-zero terms?** - A polynomial like $5x^{1000} + 3$ has degree 1000 but only **2 non-zero terms**. - Dense array would waste 999 zero cells. - Sparse representation stores only the 2 useful terms. **How does polynomial addition work?** For two polynomials $P_1(x)$ and $P_2(x)$, addition is defined as: $$R(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x) = \sum_{i} (a_i + b_i) x^i$$ This means we walk through the two term-lists and: - If exponents are **equal** → add coefficients. - If exponents are **different** → copy the term with the higher exponent to the result. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application | |---|---|---| | Polynomial form | $P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i x^i$ | General form | | Degree of polynomial | $\max(\text{expo}_i)$ | Highest exponent with non-zero coeff | | Number of terms | $k = \text{count of } a_i \neq 0$ | Length of sparse array | | Addition: same exponent | $a_i + b_i$ | When $\text{expo}_1 = \text{expo}_2$ | | Addition: diff exponent | Copy term with $\max(\text{expo})$ | Preserves descending order | | Time complexity (addition) | $O(m + n)$ | $m, n$ = number of terms in $P_1, P_2$ | | Space complexity (result) | $O(m + n)$ | Worst case all terms combine | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Polynomial representation is foundational in: > 1. **Signal Processing** — FIR/IIR digital filters are polynomials in $z^{-1}$. > 2. **Computer Graphics** — Bezier curves and splines use polynomial basis functions. > 3. **Cryptography** — Polynomial arithmetic over finite fields (AES-GCM uses GF(2^128) polynomials). > 4. **Compiler Design** — Expression trees simplify to polynomial form for optimization. > 5. **Scientific Computing** — Taylor series approximations are stored as polynomial terms. ### 2.5 Ordering Convention > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU standard convention: Terms are stored in **strictly descending order of exponents** in the array. This enables the **two-pointer merge technique** for polynomial addition in linear time. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: Polynomial Addition **Given:** $$P_1(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 7$$ $$P_2(x) = 4x^4 + 2x^3 + 9x^2 + 1$$ **Find:** $R(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$ #### Step 1: Represent each polynomial as a sparse array of terms $$\text{Array}_1 = [\{5, 4\}, \{3, 2\}, \{7, 0\}] \quad (n_1 = 3 \text{ terms})$$ $$\text{Array}_2 = [\{4, 4\}, \{2, 3\}, \{9, 2\}, \{1, 0\}] \quad (n_2 = 4 \text{ terms})$$ #### Step 2: Initialize result array with maximum possible size $$\text{Size}_R = n_1 + n_2 = 3 + 4 = 7$$ #### Step 3: Two-Pointer Merge Algorithm Let $i = 0$ (pointer for $P_1$), $j = 0$ (pointer for $P_2$), $k = 0$ (pointer for $R$). **Iteration 1:** Compare $\text{expo}_1[i]$ vs $\text{expo}_2[j]$ $$\text{expo}_1[0] = 4, \quad \text{expo}_2[0] = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Equal!}$$ Compute new coefficient: $$c = 5 + 4 = 9$$ Append to result: $$R = [\{9, 4\}]$$ $$i = 1, \quad j = 1, \quad k = 1$$ **Iteration 2:** Compare $\text{expo}_1[1] = 2$ vs $\text{expo}_2[1] = 3$ $$\text{expo}_2[1] > \text{expo}_1[1] \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Copy term from } P_2$$ $$R = [\{9, 4\}, \{2, 3\}]$$ $$j = 2, \quad k = 2$$ **Iteration 3:** Compare $\text{expo}_1[1] = 2$ vs $\text{expo}_2[2] = 2$ → Equal! $$c = 3 + 9 = 12$$ $$R = [\{9, 4\}, \{2, 3\}, \{12, 2\}]$$ $$i = 2, \quad j = 3, \quad k = 3$$ **Iteration 4:** Compare $\text{expo}_1[2] = 0$ vs $\text{expo}_2[3] = 1$ $$\text{expo}_2[3] > \text{expo}_1[2] \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Copy from } P_2$$ $$R = [\{9, 4\}, \{2, 3\}, \{12, 2\}, \{1, 1\}]$$ $$j = 4, \quad k = 4$$ **Iteration 5:** $j = 4$ has reached end of $P_2$. Copy remaining terms of $P_1$ to $R$. $$R = [\{9, 4\}, \{2, 3\}, \{12, 2\}, \{1, 1\}, \{7, 0\}]$$ $$k = 5$$ #### Step 4: Final Result $$\boxed{R(x) = 9x^4 + 2x^3 + 12x^2 + x + 7}$$ ### 3.2 Complete KTU-Standard C Program ```c #include #define MAX 50 // Structure to hold a single polynomial term struct Term { int coeff; // Coefficient of the term int expo; // Exponent (power) of x }; // Global arrays to hold polynomials struct Term P1[MAX], P2[MAX], R[MAX]; int n1 = 0, n2 = 0, nR = 0; // Term counts // Function to read a polynomial void readPoly(struct Term P[], int *n) { printf("Enter number of non-zero terms: "); scanf("%d", n); printf("Enter terms in DESCENDING order of exponent:\n"); for (int i = 0; i < *n; i++) { printf(" Term %d -> coefficient: ", i + 1); scanf("%d", &P[i].coeff); printf(" Term %d -> exponent : ", i + 1); scanf("%d", &P[i].expo); } } // Function to display a polynomial void displayPoly(struct Term P[], int n) { for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { if (P[i].coeff != 0) { if (i != 0 && P[i].coeff > 0) printf(" + "); else if (P[i].coeff < 0) printf(" - "); printf("%d", (P[i].coeff > 0) ? P[i].coeff : -P[i].coeff); if (P[i].expo != 0) printf("x^%d", P[i].expo); } } printf("\n"); } // Core Algorithm: Polynomial Addition using Two-Pointer Merge void addPoly(struct Term P1[], int n1, struct Term P2[], int n2, struct Term R[], int *nR) { int i = 0, j = 0, k = 0; // Main merge loop while (i < n1 && j < n2) { if (P1[i].expo == P2[j].expo) { // Same exponent: add coefficients int sumCoeff = P1[i].coeff + P2[j].coeff; if (sumCoeff != 0) { // Avoid storing zero terms R[k].coeff = sumCoeff; R[k].expo = P1[i].expo; k++; } i++; j++; } else if (P1[i].expo > P2[j].expo) { // P1's exponent is larger: copy P1's term R[k] = P1[i]; i++; k++; } else { // P2's exponent is larger: copy P2's term R[k] = P2[j]; j++; k++; } } // Copy any remaining terms of P1 while (i < n1) { R[k] = P1[i]; i++; k++; } // Copy any remaining terms of P2 while (j < n2) { R[k] = P2[j]; j++; k++; } *nR = k; // Update result term count } int main() { printf("=== Polynomial Representation & Addition ===\n\n"); printf("Enter first polynomial:\n"); readPoly(P1, &n1); printf("\nEnter second polynomial:\n"); readPoly(P2, &n2); printf("\nFirst polynomial : "); displayPoly(P1, n1); printf("Second polynomial : "); displayPoly(P2, n2); addPoly(P1, n1, P2, n2, R, &nR); printf("----------------------------\n"); printf("Resultant polynomial: "); displayPoly(R, nR); return 0; } ``` ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Trace of the Algorithm For the input polynomials: - $P_1 = \{5,4\}, \{3,2\}, \{7,0\}$ → $n_1 = 3$ - $P_2 = \{4,4\}, \{2,3\}, \{9,2\}, \{1,0\}$ → $n_2 = 4$ | Step | $i$ | $j$ | $\text{P1.expo}$ | $\text{P2.expo}$ | Action | Result Array $R$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Init | 0 | 0 | — | — | $k = 0$ | [ ] | | 1 | 0 | 0 | 4 | 4 | Equal → $5+4=9$ | $[\{9,4\}]$ | | 2 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 4 | $\text{P2} > \text{P1}$ → copy P2 | $[\{9,4\}, \{2,3\}]$ | | 3 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | Equal → $3+9=12$ | $[\{9,4\}, \{2,3\}, \{12,2\}]$ | | 4 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 2 | $\text{P2} > \text{P1}$ → copy P2 | $[\{9,4\}, \{2,3\}, \{12,2\}, \{1,1\}]$ | | 5 | 2 | 1 | 0 | — | Copy remaining P1 | $[\{9,4\}, \{2,3\}, \{12,2\}, \{1,1\}, \{7,0\}]$ | ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Properties | Property | Value | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Time Complexity** | $O(m + n)$ | Each term visited at most once | | **Space Complexity** | $O(m + n)$ | Result holds at most all terms | | **In-place?** | No | Uses separate result array | | **Stable?** | Yes | Preserves order from inputs | | **Handles zero result?** | Yes | Skips zero-coefficient terms | | **Requires sorted input?** | Yes (descending) | Enables single-pass merge | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Program Start]) --> B[Declare Term Structure: coeff, expo] B --> C[Declare Global Arrays: P1, P2, R] C --> D[Read P1: n1 terms] D --> E[Read P2: n2 terms] E --> F[Display P1] F --> G[Display P2] G --> H[Call addPoly Function] H --> I[Initialize i=0, j=0, k=0] I --> J{i less than n1 AND j less than n2} J -->|Yes| K{P1 expo equals P2 expo} J -->|No| L[Copy remaining terms of P1] K -->|Yes| M[Sum coeffs of P1 and P2] M --> N{Is sumCoeff not equal 0} N -->|Yes| O[Store term in R, increment k] N -->|No| P[Skip zero term] O --> Q[i++, j++] P --> Q K -->|No| R{P1 expo greater than P2 expo} R -->|Yes| S[Copy P1 term to R, i++, k++] R -->|No| T[Copy P2 term to R, j++, k++] Q --> J S --> J T --> J L --> U[Copy remaining terms of P2] U --> V[Set nR = k] V --> W[Display Resultant Polynomial R] W --> X([Program End]) ``` ### 4.2 Array Memory Layout (Sparse Representation) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph P1["P1 Array (n1 = 3)"] A1["Index 0
coeff = 5
expo = 4"] A2["Index 1
coeff = 3
expo = 2"] A3["Index 2
coeff = 7
expo = 0"] end subgraph P2["P2 Array (n2 = 4)"] B1["Index 0
coeff = 4
expo = 4"] B2["Index 1
coeff = 2
expo = 3"] B3["Index 2
coeff = 9
expo = 2"] B4["Index 3
coeff = 1
expo = 0"] end subgraph RES["Result Array R (nR = 5)"] C1["Index 0
coeff = 9
expo = 4"] C2["Index 1
coeff = 2
expo = 3"] C3["Index 2
coeff = 12
expo = 2"] C4["Index 3
coeff = 1
expo = 1"] C5["Index 4
coeff = 7
expo = 0"] end ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree for Polynomial Addition ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Two terms to merge: T1, T2]) --> Check{E1 vs E2
Compare Exponents} Check -->|E1 greater than E2| Path1[Choose T1 for result
Move pointer in P1] Check -->|E1 less than E2| Path2[Choose T2 for result
Move pointer in P2] Check -->|E1 equals E2| Path3[Add coefficients
Check if sum is zero] Path3 --> ZeroCheck{Sum equals 0?} ZeroCheck -->|Yes| Skip[Skip this term
Move both pointers] ZeroCheck -->|No| Create[Create new term with E1
and sum coefficient] Path1 --> Next([Loop continues]) Path2 --> Next Create --> Next Skip --> Next ``` ### 4.4 System Architecture for the Experiment ```mermaid graph TB Input[User Input: Terms of P1, P2] --> Validator[Input Validator
Sorted Descending?] Validator -->|Valid| Storage[Term Storage Arrays] Validator -->|Invalid| Reject[Reject and Re-prompt] Reject --> Input Storage --> AddModule[Polynomial Addition Module] AddModule --> MergeEngine[Two-Pointer Merge Engine] MergeEngine --> ResultBuilder[Result Term Builder] ResultBuilder --> Output[Formatted Output Display] Output --> Eval[Evaluation: O m plus n] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is polynomial representation using arrays? Explain the sparse representation with an example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Polynomial representation using arrays is a memory-efficient technique to store a polynomial $P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + a_0$ in a one-dimensional array. There are two main approaches: 1. **Dense Representation:** Uses an array of size $n+1$ where index $i$ stores the coefficient $a_i$. Wastes memory for zero terms. 2. **Sparse Representation:** Uses an array of structures storing only non-zero terms. Each element contains $\{ \text{coefficient}, \text{exponent} \}$. **Example:** For $P(x) = 6x^5 + 4x^2 + 9$, the sparse array is: $$\text{Poly} = [\{6, 5\}, \{4, 2\}, \{9, 0\}]$$ This uses only 3 cells instead of 6, saving memory. Terms are stored in descending order of exponents for efficient addition. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Write the time complexity of polynomial addition using arrays. Justify your answer.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The time complexity of polynomial addition using the array representation with the two-pointer merge technique is **$O(m + n)$**, where $m$ and $n$ are the number of non-zero terms in the two input polynomials. **Justification:** The algorithm uses two pointers, $i$ and $j$, which traverse the two arrays exactly once. In the worst case: - Each iteration either advances $i$, $j$, or both. - Total pointer movements = at most $m + n$ steps. - Each step performs only constant-time operations (comparison, addition, or copy). Hence, the total time is linear in the total number of terms: $T(m, n) = O(m + n)$. The space complexity of the result is also $O(m + n)$. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Represent the polynomials $P_1(x) = 8x^4 + 5x^2 + 3$ and $P_2(x) = 6x^4 + 2x^3 + 4x^2 + 7$ using arrays. Show the structure definition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete C program to perform the addition of two polynomials represented as sparse arrays. Display the resultant polynomial in standard mathematical form. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Structure Definition (2 marks):** ```c #define MAX 50 struct Term { int coeff; int expo; }; ``` **Representation (5 marks):** For $P_1(x) = 8x^4 + 5x^2 + 3$ (3 non-zero terms): | Index | coeff | expo | |---|---|---| | 0 | 8 | 4 | | 1 | 5 | 2 | | 2 | 3 | 0 | For $P_2(x) = 6x^4 + 2x^3 + 4x^2 + 7$ (4 non-zero terms): | Index | coeff | expo | |---|---|---| | 0 | 6 | 4 | | 1 | 2 | 3 | | 2 | 4 | 2 | | 3 | 7 | 0 | Storage uses only $3 + 4 = 7$ cells instead of $5 + 4 = 9$ for dense representation, demonstrating memory efficiency. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Complete C Program (7 marks):** ```c #include #define MAX 50 struct Term { int coeff; int expo; }; struct Term P1[MAX], P2[MAX], R[MAX]; int n1, n2, nR; void readPoly(struct Term P[], int *n) { printf("Enter number of terms: "); scanf("%d", n); for (int i = 0; i < *n; i++) { printf("Coeff, Expo: "); scanf("%d %d", &P[i].coeff, &P[i].expo); } } void display(struct Term P[], int n) { for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { if (i != 0 && P[i].coeff > 0) printf(" + "); else if (P[i].coeff < 0) printf(" - "); printf("%d", (P[i].coeff > 0) ? P[i].coeff : -P[i].coeff); if (P[i].expo != 0) printf("x^%d", P[i].expo); } printf("\n"); } void addPoly() { int i = 0, j = 0, k = 0; while (i < n1 && j < n2) { if (P1[i].expo == P2[j].expo) { int sum = P1[i].coeff + P2[j].coeff; if (sum != 0) { R[k].coeff = sum; R[k].expo = P1[i].expo; k++; } i++; j++; } else if (P1[i].expo > P2[j].expo) { R[k++] = P1[i++]; } else { R[k++] = P2[j++]; } } while (i < n1) R[k++] = P1[i++]; while (j < n2) R[k++] = P2[j++]; nR = k; } int main() { printf("P1: "); readPoly(P1, &n1); printf("P2: "); readPoly(P2, &n2); addPoly(); printf("Sum: "); display(R, nR); return 0; } ``` **Output for given inputs:** $$R(x) = 14x^4 + 2x^3 + 9x^2 + 10$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the concept of polynomial addition. State the rules used when adding two polynomials represented as sparse arrays. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two polynomials $A(x) = 7x^5 + 4x^3 + 2x + 5$ and $B(x) = 3x^5 + 6x^4 + 4x^3 + x^2$ are represented as sparse arrays. Perform the addition $A(x) + B(x)$ step-by-step showing all iterations, and write the final resultant polynomial. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Concept (3 marks):** Polynomial addition combines like terms (terms with the same exponent) from two polynomials. Given $P_1(x) = \sum a_i x^i$ and $P_2(x) = \sum b_i x^i$, the result is $R(x) = \sum (a_i + b_i) x^i$. **Rules for Sparse Array Addition (4 marks):** 1. **Equal exponents:** If $\text{expo}_1[i] = \text{expo}_2[j]$, add the coefficients. If sum is zero, skip the term. 2. **Different exponents (P1 larger):** If $\text{expo}_1[i] > \text{expo}_2[j]$, copy term from $P_1$ to result. 3. **Different exponents (P2 larger):** If $\text{expo}_1[i] < \text{expo}_2[j]$, copy term from $P_2$ to result. 4. **Pointer advancement:** Always advance the pointer of the array from which a term was consumed. 5. **Drain remaining terms:** After one array is exhausted, copy all remaining terms from the other array. 6. **Sorted order requirement:** Input arrays must be in descending order of exponents for the algorithm to work correctly. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Initial Setup (1 mark):** - $A(x)$ sparse array: $[\{7,5\}, \{4,3\}, \{2,1\}, \{5,0\}]$, $n_A = 4$ - $B(x)$ sparse array: $[\{3,5\}, \{6,4\}, \{4,3\}, \{1,2\}]$, $n_B = 4$ **Step-by-Step Iteration (5 marks):** | Step | $i$ | $j$ | $A.\text{expo}$ | $B.\text{expo}$ | Action | Result $R$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0 | 5 | 5 | Equal: $7 + 3 = 10$ | $[\{10, 5\}]$ | | 2 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 5 | $B$ larger → copy $B$ | $[\{10,5\}, \{6,4\}]$ | | 3 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 3 | Equal: $4 + 4 = 8$ | $[\{10,5\}, \{6,4\}, \{8,3\}]$ | | 4 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 4 | $B$ larger → copy $B$ | $[\{10,5\}, \{6,4\}, \{8,3\}, \{1,2\}]$ | | 5 | 2 | 2 | 1 | — | Drain $A$: copy remaining | $[\{10,5\}, \{6,4\}, \{8,3\}, \{1,2\}, \{2,1\}, \{5,0\}]$ | **Final Result (1 mark):** $$\boxed{R(x) = 10x^5 + 6x^4 + 8x^3 + x^2 + 2x + 5}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab Exam:** > 1. **Forgetting to skip zero-sum terms** — When two terms have the same exponent but their coefficients sum to zero, students often add an entry to the result with coefficient 0, which is incorrect. Always check `if (sum != 0)` before storing. > 2. **Not draining remaining terms** — Students stop the loop when $i \ge n_1$ or $j \ge n_2$ but forget to copy the leftover terms from the non-exhausted array. This causes the result to be incomplete and loses **2–3 marks**. > 3. **Wrong sign handling in `display()`** — Not handling the `+` vs `-` separator correctly when printing negative coefficients. The standard pattern: if $i \neq 0$ and coeff $> 0$, print `+`; if coeff $< 0$, print `-` and use absolute value. > 4. **Assuming input is sorted** — The algorithm only works if both input arrays are in **strictly descending order of exponents**. Students lose marks by not stating this precondition explicitly. > 5. **Confusing dense vs sparse representation** — KTU Module 2 expects sparse (terms only), not dense (array index = exponent). State your representation choice in the viva. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Lab Exam 2:** > - **Structure:** `struct Term { int coeff; int expo; };` is mandatory. > - **Sparse representation** stores only non-zero terms in **descending order of exponent**. > - **Polynomial addition** uses the **two-pointer merge technique**. > - **Time complexity** is $O(m + n)$; **space complexity** of result is $O(m + n)$. > - **Three rules of merge:** equal exponents → add coefficients; larger exponent → copy that term; never leave leftover terms uncopied. > - **Skip zero-sum terms** explicitly: if `coeff1 + coeff2 == 0`, do not store. > - **Display function** must handle signs, absolute values, and the constant term (exponent 0). > - **Always pass by reference** for term count using pointers in C. > - **Test cases to remember:** polynomials with equal degree, different degrees, missing terms, and terms summing to zero. > - **Real-world link:** Polynomial representation underlies signal filters, cryptography, and computer graphics curves.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) PCITL308 – Data Structures Lab**, *searching* is formally defined as the algorithmic process of determining whether a given **key element (target)** exists within a finite, organized collection of data items (such as an array, list, or tree), and if it does, retrieving its precise positional index.

For **Module Experiment 2**, the syllabus restricts the scope to two foundational, comparison-based, deterministic algorithms:

1. **Linear Search (Sequential Search):** A brute-force, order-agnostic algorithm that scans each element of the data structure one by one until the target is found or the collection is exhausted.
2. **Binary Search (Logarithmic Search / Half-Interval Search):** A divide-and-conquer algorithm that requires the underlying collection to be **sorted in monotonic order** (ascending or descending). It repeatedly halves the search space by comparing the target with the middle element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> This experiment is mapped to **CO2 – Apply algorithmic strategies for searching and sorting in real-world problem solving** under **Bloom's Level: Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)**. The expected outcome is that students can *implement, trace, and tabulate* the iterative behavior of both algorithms.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Linear Search = "Looking for a Surname in an Unruled Notebook"**
> Imagine you have a stack of 100 attendance sheets that are completely unsorted, and your professor asks you to find whether "Rahul M." was present today. You have no choice but to start from **Sheet 1, Row 1** and flip through every single page, comparing every name with "Rahul M.", until you either find him or finish the entire stack. This is **Linear Search** — *exhaustive, simple, but slow on large data.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Binary Search = "Finding a Word in a Dictionary"**
> You want to find the word "Algorithm" in a 1,000-page English dictionary. Would you scan from page 1? No! You open roughly the middle (page 500). If the word on that page is alphabetically *after* "Algorithm", you ignore the right half; if *before*, you ignore the left half. You repeat this halving. You will find the word in about **10 steps** (because $2^{10} = 1024 \ge 1000$). This is **Binary Search** — *logarithmic, blazing fast, but demands sorted data.*

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

The two algorithms are benchmarked using the following standard, board-exam-standard metrics:

- **Time Complexity (T(n)):** A function expressing the number of elementary comparison operations as a function of input size $n$.
- **Space Complexity (S(n)):** Auxiliary memory used beyond the input array.
- **Best Case (Ω):** Fastest possible execution for an optimal input arrangement.
- **Average Case (Θ):** Expected behavior over all possible inputs.
- **Worst Case (O):** Maximum number of comparisons for a pathological input.
- **Number of Comparisons (C):** The count of key-to-array-element comparisons executed.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The single most-frequently asked question on this experiment is *"Compare Linear Search and Binary Search."* Always tabulate the comparison; never write it as a paragraph.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Search — Operational Logic

Linear Search is a **non-recursive, sequential** procedure. The formal pseudocode logic is:

1. **Initialize** an index variable `i` to 0.
2. **Iterate** while `i < n` (where `n` is the array length):
    - **Compare** the key `K` with the array element `A[i]`.
    - **If** `A[i] == K`, declare success and return index `i`.
    - **Else**, increment `i` by 1 and continue.
3. **If** the loop terminates without a match, return a sentinel value (typically `-1`) to indicate failure.

**Why does it work on unsorted data?** Because the algorithm makes no assumption about the distribution or order of elements. It performs a *brute-force* exhaustive scan, so correctness is guaranteed for **any** arrangement.

**How does it terminate?**
- **Successful termination:** Triggered by a matching comparison.
- **Unsuccessful termination:** Triggered by exhausting the index range.

---

## 2.2 Binary Search — Operational Logic (The Divide-and-Conquer Engine)

Binary Search is a **divide-and-conquer, recursive or iterative** procedure with one strict precondition: **The array must be sorted in ascending (or descending) order.**

1. Set two boundary indices: `low = 0` and `high = n - 1`.
2. **Repeat** while `low <= high`:
    - Compute the **midpoint index** as `mid = low + (high - low) / 2`. *(The KTU-preferred formulation to prevent integer overflow in languages like C/Java.)*
    - **Compare** the key `K` with the array element `A[mid]`:
        - **Case A — Match:** If `A[mid] == K`, return `mid`.
        - **Case B — Left Subarray:** If `A[mid] > K` (and the array is ascending), set `high = mid - 1` and discard the right half.
        - **Case C — Right Subarray:** If `A[mid] < K`, set `low = mid + 1` and discard the left half.
3. **If** the loop terminates with `low > high`, return `-1` (not found).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `low + (high - low) / 2` instead of `(low + high) / 2`?**
> In KTU lab viva voce, a very common follow-up question. The latter formulation `(low + high) / 2` can suffer from **integer overflow** when `low` and `high` are very large values (e.g., near $2^{31}$). The corrected form is mathematically equivalent:
> $$\text{low} + \frac{\text{high} - \text{low}}{2} = \frac{2\cdot\text{low} + \text{high} - \text{low}}{2} = \frac{\text{low} + \text{high}}{2}$$
> The KTU-preferred formulation is **defensive programming**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is a **high-yield, exam-ready summary** of every formula, recurrence, and boundary condition you will need.

| **Metric / Property** | **Linear Search** | **Binary Search** |
|---|---|---|
| Precondition on Data | **None (works on unsorted data)** | **Must be sorted (ascending or descending)** |
| Best Case Complexity | $\Omega(1)$ — target is at index 0 | $\Omega(1)$ — target is at midpoint on first probe |
| Average Case Complexity | $\Theta\!\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right) \approx \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(\log_2 n)$ |
| Worst Case Complexity | $O(n)$ — target absent or at last index | $O(\log_2 n)$ — target absent or at leaf of decision tree |
| Recurrence Relation | $T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1)$ | $T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1)$ |
| Closed-Form (Master Theorem) | $T(n) = n$ | $T(n) = \log_2 n + 1$ |
| Number of Comparisons (Worst) | $C_{\max} = n$ | $C_{\max} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ |
| Auxiliary Space | $O(1)$ — in-place iteration | $O(1)$ iterative, $O(\log n)$ recursive (call stack) |
| Algorithm Class | Brute-force / Sequential | Divide-and-Conquer |
| Stable? | Yes (preserves order of duplicates) | Yes |
| Suitable Data Structures | Array, Linked List, Unsorted Vector | Sorted Array, Sorted Vector, BST |
| Failure Sentinel | Returns $-1$ | Returns $-1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Theorem Reference (Board Exam Standard):**
> For Binary Search's recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)$, apply the Master Theorem with $a=1$, $b=2$, $f(n) = O(1)$.
> - $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 1} = n^0 = 1$.
> - Since $f(n) = \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^0)$, we are in **Case 2** of the Master Theorem.
> - Therefore: $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$. ✅

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linear Search:** Used in **small datasets (n < 50)**, **unsorted streaming data**, **firewall packet inspection** (when rules are unsorted and latency per packet must be bounded), and **debugging probes**.
- **Binary Search:** Used in **database indexing (B-Trees, B+ Trees)**, **dictionary implementations**, **git bisect** (debugging), **Dijkstra's priority queue lookups**, **compiler symbol tables**, and **routing table lookups in network protocols**.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Lab Record:** Always include a *Complexity Analysis* section at the end of your record. The examiner will allocate **2 of 5 marks** for the proper derivation of time complexity.
